caveats: a: BLS reports the broader NAICS 621400 outpatient-center workforce rather than 621410 and provides employment shares rather than wage-weighted task hours.
a: O*NET task descriptions are cross-industry occupational profiles and do not identify tasks already automated in family planning centers.
a: The six-digit industry mixes counseling-led clinics, fertility centers, and procedure-intensive services with materially different task exposure.
rho: No representative survey measures AI implementation or realized labor-hour reduction in NAICS 621410.
rho: Digitized forms, portal messaging, and scheduling are workflow automation and are not necessarily AI.
rho: Large networks may deploy integrated tools faster than independent or grant-dependent clinics.
e: No representative firm-level source measures the combined recurrence, control, transferability, concentration, compliance, and normalized-earnings screens.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a broad healthcare-facilities margin and may misclassify specialized fertility and grant-supported centers.
e: The industry definition combines business models with different payer, ownership, and regulatory structures.
s5: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying healthcare transactions.
s5: Family planning ownership, tax status, and regulatory conditions may make the broad employer-business proxy systematically high.
s5: No source measures five-year control transfers specifically for 621410.
q: Medicaid's coverage requirement establishes a major administered-payment channel but does not measure the payer mix or retained automation benefit of 621410 firms.
q: No representative source reports family planning contract repricing, self-pay competition, grant treatment of savings, or post-automation margins.
q: Retention varies sharply between grant-supported contraceptive clinics and self-pay fertility services.
d5: CDC measures national use and receipt of services across all provider settings, not demand at NAICS 621410 centers.
d5: The current service basket includes contraception, fertility, counseling, and procedures with different demand paths.
d5: Recent utilization snapshots do not establish a five-year constant-price trend, so this primitive remains an estimate.
o: Service definitions and occupation tasks establish accountable clinical work but do not measure the fraction that will still require a dedicated center in year five.
o: Telehealth may preserve a licensed operator while eliminating some physical-site demand.
o: Substitution differs materially between counseling or prescription visits and device, specimen, fertility, sterilization, or other procedural services.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patient-service revenue paid through Medicaid, commercial insurance, self-pay, grants, and program funding for consultations, examinations, testing, counseling, prescriptions, procedures, fertility services, and related follow-up care.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospital departments, captive health-system units, physician offices classified elsewhere, product-only sellers, shell entities, non-control financing situations, and centers without transferable operations or normalized EBITDA in the target band.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market family planning centers providing recurring or repeat outpatient contraceptive, fertility, pregnancy, genetic, prenatal, sterilization, and medically indicated reproductive-health services to external patients.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS occupational employment for broad NAICS 621400 and O*NET tasks for medical assistants and registered nurses, interpreted with the official 621410 service definition. Transformation: assigned greater exposure to records, scheduling, billing, intake, follow-up, routine education, and documentation, and lower exposure to licensed judgment, physical examination, specimens, medication, procedures, and safeguarding. Error direction: broad outpatient-center data overweights specialties such as dialysis and surgery relative to family planning, while omission of clerical work embedded in clinical roles may bias exposure downward.
caveats: BLS reports the broader NAICS 621400 outpatient-center workforce rather than 621410 and provides employment shares rather than wage-weighted task hours.
O*NET task descriptions are cross-industry occupational profiles and do not identify tasks already automated in family planning centers.
The six-digit industry mixes counseling-led clinics, fertility centers, and procedure-intensive services with materially different task exposure.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study from multi-site family planning operators, separating administrative, counseling, diagnostic, procedural, laboratory, and supervisory hours before and after ambient documentation and automated revenue-cycle deployment, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Scheduling, eligibility checks, intake, history capture, coding, billing, prior authorization, results communication, documentation, outreach, and routine education are exposed to AI assistance or avoidable hiring. Examinations, specimen collection, medication administration, device placement, procedures, sensitive counseling, and clinical accountability remain substantially human.

caveats: CDC measures national use and receipt of services across all provider settings, not demand at NAICS 621410 centers.
The current service basket includes contraception, fertility, counseling, and procedures with different demand paths.
Recent utilization snapshots do not establish a five-year constant-price trend, so this primitive remains an estimate.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price encounter and procedure histories from FPAR, commercial claims, Medicaid T-MSIS, and multi-site family planning and fertility operators, reconciled for site entry and policy changes, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Family planning remains a broadly used outpatient service, with recurring needs for contraception, prescriptions, testing, counseling, fertility support, and procedures. Demographics, insurance and grant policy, state restrictions, alternative primary-care provision, long-acting methods, and changing fertility intentions create offsetting forces, so the base assumes approximately stable real service-basket demand.

caveats: No representative firm-level source measures the combined recurrence, control, transferability, concentration, compliance, and normalized-earnings screens.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a broad healthcare-facilities margin and may misclassify specialized fertility and grant-supported centers.
The industry definition combines business models with different payer, ownership, and regulatory structures.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level ownership and revenue census covering tax status, health-system affiliation, service mix, payer and grant mix, clinician concentration, licenses, normalized EBITDA, and change-of-control requirements would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The service is outsourced to external patients and often produces repeat visits, follow-up, prescriptions, and procedures. Eligibility is reduced by nonprofit or public control, grant restrictions, hospital affiliation, highly specialized founder-dependent practice, adverse payer mix, political and regulatory exposure, weak transferability, and economics outside the target band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621410 ($2,031,767,251) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621410 ($4,734,655,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $160,529 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621410); class <5: 812 firms, avg revenue/firm $282,030 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $44,561 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 400 firms, avg revenue/firm $755,118 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $119,309 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 250 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,445,732 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $228,426 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 172 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,684,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $898,112 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 82 firms, avg revenue/firm $23,744,829 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,751,683 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,764,286 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,176,757 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 110

o.bridge: Source population/quantity: the official 621410 service definition, CDC evidence on services received from medical providers, and O*NET clinical and administrative tasks. Transformation: assigned continued operator need to prescribing, examination, specimens, diagnostics, devices, procedures, consent, and clinical escalation, while discounting for digital education, intake, follow-up, telehealth, pharmacy and primary-care substitution, and home testing. Error direction: using current clinical workflows may overstate operator need as remote care expands, while privacy, safety, licensing, and procedure requirements may keep operator dependence higher.
caveats: Service definitions and occupation tasks establish accountable clinical work but do not measure the fraction that will still require a dedicated center in year five.
Telehealth may preserve a licensed operator while eliminating some physical-site demand.
Substitution differs materially between counseling or prescription visits and device, specimen, fertility, sterilization, or other procedural services.
o.change_evidence: Patient-cohort data tracking migration from dedicated centers to digital-only contraception, primary care, pharmacy prescribing, home testing, and self-service fertility tools, together with outcomes and licensed-review requirements, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Software can self-serve education, screening, scheduling, history collection, reminders, and some counseling, but most of the current basket still requires a licensed accountable provider for individualized prescribing, examination, specimens, testing, devices, procedures, consent, and escalation. Primary-care substitution and digital contraceptive services can bypass some dedicated-center volume.

caveats: Medicaid's coverage requirement establishes a major administered-payment channel but does not measure the payer mix or retained automation benefit of 621410 firms.
No representative source reports family planning contract repricing, self-pay competition, grant treatment of savings, or post-automation margins.
Retention varies sharply between grant-supported contraceptive clinics and self-pay fertility services.
q.change_evidence: Matched clinic-level gross-margin and reimbursement data before and after automation, separated by Medicaid, commercial insurance, self-pay, Title X or other grants, fertility services, and procedures, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Centers can retain some administrative productivity between payer-contract resets and in self-pay or grant-funded operations, particularly through additional capacity and reduced denials. Retention is limited by administered Medicaid rates, insurer negotiation, grant budgets, patient affordability, competitive fertility pricing, required staffing, and pressure to convert efficiency into access.

caveats: No representative survey measures AI implementation or realized labor-hour reduction in NAICS 621410.
Digitized forms, portal messaging, and scheduling are workflow automation and are not necessarily AI.
Large networks may deploy integrated tools faster than independent or grant-dependent clinics.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation and labor-hour data from Planned Parenthood affiliates, Title X networks, independent fertility groups, and other multi-site family planning operators using ambient documentation, automated intake, coding, and patient-communication tools would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years permits meaningful use of ambient notes, automated intake, eligibility and coding support, patient messaging, scheduling, and care-gap outreach. Implementation is restrained by fragmented funding, small-site integration capacity, privacy, clinical validation, state-specific rules, sensitive communications, and the need for licensed review.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup's five-year sale-or-transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners. Transformation: used the reported 22% intention rate as an upper anchor and discounted for noncompletion, internal or family transfers, nonprofit and public ownership, licensure, payer enrollment, grant restrictions, clinician dependence, and diligence failures. Error direction: intentions overstate completed qualifying control sales, while consolidation among transferable fertility and reproductive-health groups could make the lower bound conservative.
caveats: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying healthcare transactions.
Family planning ownership, tax status, and regulatory conditions may make the broad employer-business proxy systematically high.
No source measures five-year control transfers specifically for 621410.
s5.change_evidence: Closed control-sale counts and eligible-center denominators from healthcare transaction databases, family planning and fertility brokers, state change-of-ownership files, and payer enrollment records over a five-year cohort would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful five-year sale or transfer intentions, but qualifying control transactions in family planning are reduced by nonprofit and public ownership, clinician dependence, licensure and payer enrollment, grant restrictions, reputation sensitivity, and transaction completion risk.

principal_driver: The principal driver is scaling recurring reproductive-health access by automating administrative and documentation work around a trusted clinical endpoint.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is a heterogeneous, policy-sensitive provider pool whose most valuable services still require licensed clinical labor and whose transferable ownership base is poorly measured.
